Questions: What does it mean to have a mental illness?
Answers: A mental illness is a health condition that disrupts a person's thoughts, emotions, relationships, and daily functioning. They are medical conditions caused by the brain's biology, not a person's character or willpower, and are treatable with medication and social support.

Questions: Who does mental illness affect?
Answers: Mental illness can affect anyone regardless of gender, age, income, ethnicity, or background. It is estimated to affect 1 in 5 adults, with 75% of mental health conditions developing by age 24.

Questions: What causes mental illness?
Answers: Mental illness is caused by a combination of genetic, biological, psychological, and environmental factors. No single cause explains all mental illnesses, and they are not caused by personal weakness or character flaws.

Questions: What are some of the warning signs of mental illness?
Answers: Common warning signs include persistent sadness or irritability, extreme mood swings, excessive anxiety or fear, social withdrawal, dramatic changes in eating or sleeping habits, difficulty coping with daily problems, and in serious cases, thoughts of suicide or self-harm.

Questions: Can people with mental illness recover?
Answers: Yes. With early identification and appropriate treatment, many people with mental illness recover fully or manage their condition effectively. Even when symptoms return, careful monitoring and treatment make it possible to live a fulfilling and productive life.

Questions: What should I do if I know someone who appears to have the symptoms of a mental disorder?
Answers: Talk to them with care and let them know you are concerned. Encourage them to seek help from a mental health professional as early as possible. If they are open to it, offer to help them find or access services, and continue to offer support without pressure.

Questions: How can I find a mental health professional for myself or my child?
Answers: Start by asking your family doctor for a referral. You can also search for licensed therapists, psychologists, or counsellors in your area through professional directories or your insurance provider. Finding the right fit may take some research.

Questions: What treatment options are available?
Answers: Treatment options include therapy (such as CBT or DBT), medication, or a combination of both. Other approaches include peer support groups, self-help programs, and lifestyle changes. Treatment is personal and what works best varies from person to person.

Questions: If I become involved in treatment, what do I need to know?
Answers: Ask your provider what is causing your condition, what treatment is recommended, how long it will take, what medications may be involved, and what your family's role will be. Being actively engaged and asking questions is key to getting the most from treatment.

Questions: What is the difference between mental health professionals?
Answers: Psychiatrists are medical doctors who can prescribe medication. Psychologists have doctoral degrees and focus on therapy. Counsellors and therapists provide talk-based support. Each has different training and areas of expertise, so the right choice depends on your needs.

Questions: How can I find a mental health professional right for my child or myself?
Answers: Ask your family doctor for a referral, or search professional directories for licensed therapists and psychologists in your area. Feeling comfortable with the professional is important, so it may take a few tries to find the right fit.

Questions: If I become involved in treatment what do I need to know?
Answers: You should ask about the diagnosis, the recommended treatment plan, medications involved and their effects, how long treatment will take, and what role your family can play. Stay actively involved and raise any concerns with your provider.

Questions: Where else can I get help?
Answers: Start by talking to someone you trust, such as a family member, doctor, or friend. You can also contact local mental health centres, peer support groups, or community organizations. Many areas have helplines and online resources available as well.

Questions: What should I know before starting a new medication?
Answers: Ask your doctor what the medication is for, when it will start working, how to take it, what side effects to expect, what to avoid while taking it, and what to do if you miss a dose. Never stop taking psychiatric medication without consulting your doctor first.

Questions: If I feel better after taking medication, does this mean I am cured and can stop taking it?
Answers: Not necessarily. Feeling better usually means the medication is working, not that the condition is cured. Stopping suddenly can cause symptoms to return or cause withdrawal effects. Always consult your doctor before making any changes to your medication.

Questions: How can I get help paying for my medication?
Answers: Some pharmaceutical companies offer prescription assistance programs for those with financial need. You may also qualify for government prescription programs. Ask your doctor or pharmacist about available options, or visit a health resources website for your region.

Questions: Where can I go to find therapy?
Answers: You can find a therapist through your family doctor's referral, online professional directories, or community mental health centres. Different types of therapy work better for different conditions, so ask about what approach suits your situation.

Questions: Where can I learn about types of mental health treatment?
Answers: Mental health conditions are treated with therapy, medication, or both. Other options include peer support, self-help programs, and complementary approaches. Talk to a mental health professional to find out which treatment is most appropriate for you.

Questions: What are the different types of mental health professionals?
Answers: The main types include psychiatrists (medical doctors who can prescribe medication), psychologists (who provide therapy and assessment), clinical counsellors, and social workers. Each has different qualifications and approaches to care.

Questions: Where can I go to find a support group?
Answers: Support groups are available through community mental health organizations, hospitals, and online platforms. Ask your mental health provider for recommendations, or search through mental health association websites for groups in your area.

Questions: Where can I go to find inpatient care?
Answers: If you or someone you know is in crisis, go to the nearest hospital emergency room or call a crisis line. Inpatient care can help people stabilize on new medications, manage severe symptoms, or get intensive support.

Questions: Where can I go to find other local services?
Answers: Your local mental health centre, community health authority, or a helpline can point you to services nearby. Online directories for mental health services in your region are also a useful starting point.

Questions: Where can I learn more information about clinical trials?
Answers: Clinical trials for mental health treatments are listed on government health websites and research institution sites. Speak with your doctor before enrolling in any trial, as they carry risks and benefits that need to be evaluated for your specific situation.

Questions: Where can I learn more information about creating a Psychiatric Advance Directive?
Answers: A Psychiatric Advance Directive is a legal document that records your treatment preferences for times when you cannot make decisions yourself. Speak with a lawyer or your mental health provider to learn how to create one in your region.

Questions: What is mental health?
Answers: Mental health refers to our emotional, psychological, and social wellbeing. It affects how we think, feel, and act, and how we handle stress, relate to others, and make decisions. Everyone has mental health, just as everyone has physical health.

Questions: What do I do if the support doesn't help?
Answers: If your current support isn't helping, let your provider know and ask about alternative approaches. Different strategies work for different people, so staying open and persistent is important. Seeking a second opinion is also a valid option.

Questions: Can you prevent mental health problems?
Answers: While not all mental health problems can be prevented, building resilience, maintaining strong social connections, managing stress, and seeking help early can reduce the risk of challenges becoming serious mental health conditions.

Questions: Are there cures for mental health problems?
Answers: Many mental health conditions can be effectively managed or resolved with the right treatment. Options like therapy, medication, exercise, good sleep, healthy nutrition, and strong social support all contribute to recovery and better mental wellbeing.

Questions: What causes mental health problems?
Answers: Mental health problems can arise from a combination of biological factors, psychological experiences, social circumstances, and life events such as trauma, loss, or chronic stress. Most conditions are caused by multiple factors working together.

Questions: What do I do if I am worried about my mental health?
Answers: Talk to someone you trust, such as a friend, family member, or doctor. It can also help to learn more about what you are experiencing. Seeking support early makes it easier to address challenges before they become more serious.

Questions: How do I know if I am unwell?
Answers: If your thoughts, feelings, or behaviours are significantly affecting your ability to function in everyday life, it is important to seek help. Signs include persistent low mood, difficulty managing daily tasks, or a noticeable change in how you think or feel.

Questions: What should I do if I am worried about a friend or relative?
Answers: Gently and kindly express your concern to them. Encourage them to seek professional support without pressuring them. Let them know you care, and offer to help them find or access services if they are ready.

Questions: How do I deal with someone telling me what to do?
Answers: Listen to advice with an open mind, but remember that you know your own situation best. It is fine to consider different perspectives while ultimately making your own informed decisions about your mental health care.

Questions: Why do I need to keep informed about health information?
Answers: Staying informed helps you make better decisions about your health and wellbeing. Reliable information reduces anxiety and helps you take appropriate action. Stick to trusted sources and limit excessive news consumption to maintain a healthy balance.

Questions: How can I tell if health information is good or bad?
Answers: Look for information from credible sources such as government health agencies, medical institutions, and qualified professionals. Be cautious of sensational headlines, anonymous sources, or advice that tries to sell you something rather than inform you.

Questions: Why do I need a plan for my mental health?
Answers: Having a plan helps you manage your mental health proactively. It allows you to set daily routines, identify helpful coping strategies, stay connected with support, and know what steps to take during difficult times before a crisis occurs.

Questions: How can I maintain social connections if I feel lonely?
Answers: Reach out to friends or family through calls, texts, or video chats. Look for community support groups or online communities with shared interests. Even small, regular contact with others can significantly reduce feelings of loneliness.

Questions: How can I take care of my physical health to support my mental health?
Answers: Eating balanced meals, exercising regularly, getting enough sleep, and limiting alcohol and drugs all support mental wellbeing. Physical and mental health are closely connected, so taking care of your body helps take care of your mind.

Questions: How can I use distraction to manage difficult thoughts or feelings?
Answers: Engaging in activities like watching TV, listening to music, reading, or working on hobbies can help shift your focus when you feel overwhelmed. Choose activities that match your energy level and that genuinely help you feel calmer.

Questions: How can I reframe negative situations to find more balanced perspectives?
Answers: Try to examine your thoughts objectively rather than assuming the worst. Ask yourself whether your interpretation is realistic, whether there is evidence against it, and whether a more balanced view exists. This can improve mood and help you find solutions.

Questions: How can I challenge negative thinking traps?
Answers: Identify the type of negative thinking you are doing, such as catastrophizing or black-and-white thinking. Look for evidence that contradicts the thought, consider how you would view the situation if it happened to a friend, and try to replace the thought with a more balanced one.

Questions: How can I manage grief?
Answers: Acknowledge and name your feelings rather than suppressing them. Give yourself time to grieve without judgment, lean on trusted people for support, maintain basic self-care like sleep and nutrition, and seek professional help if grief becomes overwhelming or prolonged.

Questions: How can I find a doctor, psychiatrist, psychologist, or counsellor?
Answers: Ask your family doctor for a referral to a specialist. For psychologists and counsellors, you can contact them directly without a referral in most cases. Professional association websites in your region also offer searchable directories to help you find the right provider.

Questions: How can I see a psychiatrist?
Answers: You typically need a referral from your family doctor to see a psychiatrist. If you do not have a regular doctor, a walk-in clinic can provide a referral. Contact your local mental health centre to learn what services are available in your area.

Questions: How can I see a psychologist?
Answers: You can contact a registered psychologist directly without a doctor's referral. Psychologists are not usually covered by basic health insurance, so you may need to pay privately or check if your workplace benefits cover the cost. Some offer sliding-scale fees.

Questions: How can I see a counsellor?
Answers: You can book an appointment with a counsellor directly without a doctor's referral. Counselling is not always covered by basic health insurance, but workplace benefits or employee assistance programs may help cover the cost. Some counsellors offer reduced fees.

Questions: How can I find a support group?
Answers: Talk to your mental health provider for recommendations. Many mental health organizations, hospitals, and community centres run support groups for various conditions. Online support groups are also available for those without local options.

Questions: Where can I find child and youth mental health services?
Answers: Start by talking to your child's school counsellor or family doctor. They can refer you to local child and youth mental health services. Many regions also have dedicated youth mental health organizations that provide assessment, therapy, and family support.

Questions: Where can older adults find help for mental health concerns?
Answers: Older adults can speak with their family doctor, contact a local mental health centre, or reach out to seniors support organizations. Mental health concerns are just as treatable in older adults, and many services are specifically designed for this age group.

Questions: Where can I find self-help materials for depression?
Answers: Self-help workbooks, online programs, and guided resources are available from mental health organizations and universities. These can help you learn coping skills and understand depression better. They work best alongside, not instead of, professional support.

Questions: Where can I find self-help materials for anxiety?
Answers: Mental health organizations like Anxiety Canada offer free toolkits, apps, and worksheets for managing anxiety. These are helpful supplements to professional treatment and can teach practical skills like relaxation techniques and cognitive strategies.

Questions: I am having a hard time coping with a physical health problem. Where can I find help?
Answers: Physical illness can significantly impact mental health. Speak with your doctor about your emotional wellbeing alongside your physical condition. Counselling, peer support programs, and self-management resources can all help you cope more effectively.

Questions: When is substance use a problem and where can I go for help?
Answers: Substance use becomes a problem when it negatively affects your health, relationships, work, or daily life. If you are concerned, speak with your doctor or contact a substance use helpline in your area for confidential advice and referrals to support services.

Questions: Where can I find free or low-cost counselling?
Answers: Low-cost counselling may be available through community mental health centres, university training clinics, employee assistance programs, or non-profit organizations. Some private counsellors offer sliding-scale fees based on income. Ask your doctor for local options.

Questions: Can I get help paying for prescription medications?
Answers: Government programs, pharmaceutical assistance plans, and some insurance plans can help cover the cost of psychiatric medications. Ask your doctor or pharmacist about programs you may qualify for based on your income or health needs.

Questions: How can I pay for mental health care?
Answers: Mental health services through public health centres are often covered by government health plans. For private services, check your workplace benefits, employee assistance programs, or ask providers about sliding-scale fees. Your doctor can guide you toward covered options.

Questions: How do I apply for income assistance if I have a mental illness?
Answers: Government income assistance programs may have categories for people with disabilities, including mental illness. Contact your local social services office or a disability advocacy organization for guidance on eligibility and the application process.

Questions: What is a medical services plan?
Answers: A medical services plan is a government-funded health insurance program that covers essential medical services such as doctor visits, specialist appointments, and hospital care. Eligibility and coverage vary by region, so check with your local health authority for details.

Questions: What is a referral in mental health care?
Answers: A referral is a formal recommendation from your doctor to see a specialist, such as a psychiatrist. It is often required to access specialist mental health services. Once a referral is made, the specialist's office will contact you with an appointment.

Questions: What is the difference between a psychiatrist and a registered psychologist?
Answers: A psychiatrist is a medical doctor who can prescribe medication and treat complex mental illnesses. A psychologist has a graduate degree in psychology and focuses on therapy and assessment but cannot prescribe medication. Both can help with mental health conditions.

Questions: What is the difference between psychotherapy and counselling?
Answers: Psychotherapy and counselling both use talk-based approaches to support mental health. Psychotherapy often involves more structured, evidence-based techniques for treating diagnosed conditions, while counselling focuses on support and problem-solving for everyday challenges.

Questions: What is the difference between CBT and DBT?
Answers: CBT (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy) helps you identify and change unhelpful thinking patterns and behaviours. DBT (Dialectical Behaviour Therapy) builds on CBT with added focus on emotional regulation, distress tolerance, and interpersonal effectiveness. Both are evidence-based and effective.

Questions: What is the difference between antidepressants?
Answers: Antidepressants are grouped into classes based on how they affect brain chemistry. Common types include SSRIs, SNRIs, and NDRIs. They differ in how they work and their side effect profiles. Your doctor will recommend the most appropriate type based on your symptoms.

Questions: I received a diagnosis but I do not think it is right. What can I do?
Answers: Bring your concerns to the professional who made the diagnosis and ask them to explain their reasoning. If you are still unsatisfied, you have the right to seek a second opinion from another qualified professional. A different perspective can help clarify your situation.

Questions: I was prescribed medication but I do not think it is working. What can I do?
Answers: Talk to the prescribing doctor about your concerns. Many medications take several weeks to show full effects. Do not stop or change your dose on your own. Your doctor can adjust the dosage, switch medications, or explore other treatment options with you.

Questions: An adult in my life seems ill and will not find help. What can I do?
Answers: Express your concerns honestly and listen to their perspective without judgment. Offer to help them access services but avoid forcing or threatening them. Take care of your own wellbeing too, and in cases of immediate danger, contact emergency services.

Questions: I am worried about my child or teenager. Where can I find help?
Answers: Start by speaking with your family doctor or your child's school counsellor, who can refer you to appropriate services. Many regions have dedicated child and youth mental health teams. Kelty Mental Health and similar organizations also offer guidance for families.

Questions: Someone I care about has a brain injury. Where can I find more information?
Answers: A brain injury can cause or worsen mental health problems. Contact a brain injury association in your area for resources and support. It can also help to find a mental health professional experienced in both brain injury and mental health conditions.

Questions: Someone I love has been diagnosed with depression. How can I help?
Answers: Listen actively and without judgment. Show empathy without trying to fix everything. Encourage them to seek or continue professional treatment. Offer practical help like companionship or assistance with daily tasks, and ask what they need rather than assuming.

Questions: Someone I love has been diagnosed with schizophrenia. How can I help?
Answers: Learn about schizophrenia from reliable sources to better understand their experience. Listen with empathy, avoid arguing about delusions or hallucinations, and ask how you can help. Support their treatment plan and take care of your own wellbeing too.

Questions: I am a young person and one of my parents has a mental illness. What can I do?
Answers: Remember that your parent's illness is not your fault. Talk to a trusted adult, school counsellor, or youth helpline for support. Taking care of your own mental health is important, and there are programs and support groups specifically for young people in your situation.

Questions: I have thoughts of suicide or someone I care about is talking about suicide. What should I do?
Answers: Take it seriously and act immediately. Call a crisis line or emergency services right away. If someone is in immediate danger, call 911. Listening with care and encouraging the person to seek professional help as soon as possible can be life-saving.

Questions: Is cannabis use harmful to young people?
Answers: Evidence suggests that the earlier and more frequently young people use cannabis, the greater the potential harm, particularly to brain development, which continues until around age 25. Young people with risk factors for mental illness should be especially cautious about cannabis use.

Questions: What is the difference between mental health and mental illness?
Answers: Mental health refers to overall emotional and psychological wellbeing, which everyone has. Mental illness is a diagnosable condition that significantly affects how a person thinks, feels, or behaves. You can have good mental health while managing a mental illness, and vice versa.

Questions: Where can I find more information about depression?
Answers: Mental health organization websites, your doctor, and reputable medical resources are good starting points. Info sheets on depression, bipolar disorder, and mood disorders can help you understand symptoms, treatment options, and how to get support.

Questions: What is the difference between anxiety and an anxiety disorder?
Answers: Anxiety is a normal reaction to stress or danger. An anxiety disorder is when anxiety becomes excessive, uncontrollable, or occurs without a clear cause, and significantly disrupts daily life. Anxiety disorders are treatable with therapy, medication, or a combination of both.

Questions: What is the difference between anxiety and stress?
Answers: Stress is usually caused by an identifiable external pressure and tends to go away once the situation resolves. Anxiety is more internal, involving persistent worry or fear that can exist even without a clear trigger and may last well beyond the original cause.

Questions: What is the difference between sadness and depression?
Answers: Sadness is a normal emotional response to difficult events and usually resolves on its own. Depression is a medical condition involving persistent low mood, loss of interest, changes in sleep or appetite, feelings of worthlessness, and difficulty functioning, lasting two weeks or more.

Questions: What is dysthymia or persistent depressive disorder?
Answers: Persistent depressive disorder, formerly called dysthymia, is a long-lasting form of depression involving fewer but ongoing symptoms for at least two years. Many people mistake it for part of their personality rather than a treatable condition, so seeking a diagnosis is important.

Questions: What is cyclothymic disorder?
Answers: Cyclothymic disorder is a mild form of bipolar disorder involving recurring episodes of hypomania and low mood that do not meet the full criteria for bipolar disorder. It is a real and treatable condition and should be assessed by a doctor or mental health professional.

Questions: What does rapid cycling mean in bipolar disorder?
Answers: Rapid cycling refers to experiencing four or more distinct mood episodes of mania or depression within a single year. It occurs in some people with bipolar disorder and can be more challenging to treat, though it often improves with proper medication and care.

Questions: Where can I find information and help for borderline personality disorder?
Answers: Borderline personality disorder is treatable, most commonly through DBT. Look for resources from mental health organizations, and speak with your doctor about a referral to a specialist with experience in personality disorders. Support groups for BPD are also available.

Questions: What is schizoid personality disorder?
Answers: Schizoid personality disorder involves a persistent pattern of detachment from social relationships and a limited range of emotional expression. People with this condition often prefer to be alone and may seem emotionally distant. It is treatable with therapy when distress is present.

Questions: What is antisocial personality disorder?
Answers: Antisocial personality disorder involves a long-standing pattern of disregarding others' rights, lack of remorse, and impulsive or reckless behaviour. It is only diagnosed in adults, though it typically begins in childhood. Psychotherapy can help reduce harmful behaviours.

Questions: What is obsessive-compulsive personality disorder?
Answers: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder involves extreme perfectionism, rigidity, and a need for control that interferes with relationships and daily life. It is different from OCD. Treatment often involves psychotherapy to improve flexibility and reduce distress.

Questions: What is binge-eating disorder?
Answers: Binge-eating disorder involves repeated episodes of eating large amounts of food rapidly, feeling out of control, and experiencing guilt or shame afterward. It is a recognised mental health condition and is treatable with therapy, support, and in some cases, medication.

Questions: What is the difference between dissociative identity disorder and schizophrenia?
Answers: Dissociative identity disorder involves fragmented identity and memory gaps, not separate personalities. Schizophrenia involves hallucinations, delusions, and disorganised thinking. They are distinct conditions with different causes, symptoms, and treatments.

Questions: What is the difference between psychosis and schizophrenia?
Answers: Psychosis is a set of symptoms including hallucinations and delusions that can occur in several conditions. Schizophrenia is a specific mental illness that causes psychosis along with other symptoms. Psychosis can also be caused by bipolar disorder, trauma, or substance use.

Questions: What are positive and negative symptoms in schizophrenia?
Answers: Positive symptoms are additions to normal experience, such as hallucinations and delusions. Negative symptoms are reductions, such as decreased motivation, emotional expression, or social engagement. Both types are part of schizophrenia and require treatment.

Questions: What is a prodrome in mental health?
Answers: A prodrome refers to early warning signs that appear before the full onset of a mental health condition, particularly psychosis. These may include changes in sleep, mood, thinking, or social behaviour. Early intervention during the prodrome stage can improve outcomes.

Questions: I am an adult and I think I might have ADHD. What can I do?
Answers: See your family doctor and discuss your concerns. They can assess your symptoms or refer you to a specialist. ADHD in adults is diagnosed through a clinical assessment and is treatable with medication, therapy, and lifestyle strategies.

Questions: What is the difference between substance use and addiction?
Answers: Substance use refers to consuming alcohol or drugs, which can range from beneficial to harmful. Addiction, or dependence, occurs when a person feels compelled to continue using a substance despite significant negative consequences to their health, relationships, or daily life.

Questions: How can I find help for an alcohol or drug use problem?
Answers: Talk to your doctor, or contact a substance use helpline for confidential advice and referrals. Many free services offer counselling, peer support, and treatment programs. Seeking help is a sign of strength, and recovery is possible with the right support.

Questions: How do I know if I am drinking too much?
Answers: Drinking becomes a problem when it negatively affects your health, relationships, work, or safety. If you often drink more than intended, feel unable to cut back, or use alcohol to cope with stress, it may be worth speaking to a doctor or counsellor.

Questions: Why is cannabis being legalised if it can be harmful?
Answers: Legalisation allows for regulation, quality control, and safer access, reducing harms associated with unregulated supply. However, legalisation does not mean cannabis is risk-free. Responsible use guidelines, particularly for young people, remain important.

Questions: How can I talk to my kids about drugs?
Answers: Have open, honest, and non-judgmental conversations. Ask them what they think and listen without lecturing. Building trust and keeping communication open is more effective than forbidding discussion. Giving accurate information helps young people make informed decisions.

Questions: What is the legal status and evidence on CBD oil?
Answers: CBD is legal in many countries and has some evidence supporting its use for pain, epilepsy, and anxiety. However, research is still developing. Speak with your doctor before using CBD, especially if you are taking other medications, as interactions are possible.

Questions: What is the evidence on vaping?
Answers: Vaping is less harmful than smoking cigarettes but is not risk-free. It exposes users to nicotine and potentially harmful chemicals. Long-term effects are still being studied. Young people are particularly vulnerable to nicotine dependence from vaping.
